segments.title: Why Winchelsea belongs in naval history
segments.summary: The opening places the 1350 Battle of Winchelsea inside the Hundred Years' War and introduces Edward III's decisive leadership role.
segments.body: The Hundred Years' War is often remembered through land battles such as Crecy, but it also produced major contests at sea. This introduction identifies Winchelsea, also called Les Espagnols sur Mer, as an English-Castilian battle fought 238 years before the Spanish Armada and frames Edward III as an unusually effective warrior-king.

evidence.note: The lecture introduces Winchelsea, its date and names, and Edward III's role.

segments.title: Gascony and the Anglo-French collision
segments.summary: Edward III's French lands, Philip VI's insecurity, and the politics of homage turn a feudal dispute over Gascony into open conflict.
segments.body: Gascony made the English king an exceptionally powerful vassal inside France, while competing dynastic claims heightened Philip VI's suspicion. Dr. Clarke explains why homage was politically dangerous for an English monarch and how both rulers came to believe they held the stronger hand.

evidence.note: The transcript connects Gascony, dynastic claims, homage, and the outbreak of conflict.

segments.title: Naval war and Edward III's message machine
segments.summary: Maritime trade and earlier victories such as Sluys mattered to the war, while Edward III ensured those successes entered public memory.
segments.body: The Edwardian campaigns depended on maritime economics as well as land raids and battles. Edward's government circulated accounts through bishops and towns, helping victories at Sluys and Winchelsea become durable examples of English success at sea.

evidence.note: The lecture links maritime trade, Sluys, and Edward III's circulation of victory reports.

segments.title: The Gascon wine trade sustained English sea power
segments.summary: Bordeaux's wine exports enriched the English crown, supported a large merchant fleet, and made uninterrupted convoys strategically essential.
segments.body: The Bordeaux trade moved tens of thousands of tons of wine and could rival the customs revenue of England itself. Gascony also depended on imported food, so convoy disruption threatened famine as well as royal finances; Bayonne's ships and seafarers consequently formed a foundation of medieval English maritime strength.

evidence.note: The transcript details Bordeaux wine revenue, food imports, convoys, and Bayonne shipping.

segments.title: Clinker cogs meet larger Castilian hulls
segments.summary: The battle matched northern clinker-built cogs against larger Iberian ships developing toward carvel construction.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke cautions against calling the Castilian vessels fully developed caravels, describing them instead as larger ships on an iterative path toward carvel construction. France hired Charles de la Cerda and his experienced Castilian force because French shipping in the Channel had been badly depleted after Sluys and subsequent English attacks.

evidence.note: The lecture compares clinker and emerging carvel construction and explains the French hire of de la Cerda.

segments.title: Flemish bases and the geography of war
segments.summary: Flanders served French naval operations much as Gascony supported England, while rivers and coasts defined how far English armies could be sustained.
segments.body: De la Cerda operated from the Flemish maritime system rather than directly from Castile. More broadly, English campaigning power was strongest near rivers and sea routes connected to home support; inland distance exposed the kingdom's manpower disadvantage and France's superior internal depth.

evidence.note: The transcript identifies Flanders as a fleet base and explains English dependence on river and sea support.

segments.title: A hired Castilian fleet turns to piracy
segments.summary: After a truce ended French pay, the Castilian force continued attacking English shipping with fortified ships and hired crossbowmen.
segments.body: The French initially paid the Castilians to blockade Channel ports, but the June truce removed that income. The fleet continued independently, fitting raised fighting platforms and crow's nests and hiring Flemish crossbowmen, though Dr. Clarke warns that royal propaganda probably exaggerated the panic it caused.

evidence.note: The transcript describes the French contract, truce, continued piracy, ship fortifications, and propaganda caveat.

segments.title: Edward III builds the battle around intelligence
segments.summary: Edward assembled about fifty ships and a dense boarding force with detailed knowledge of the Castilian fleet's strength and dispersed approach.
segments.body: The English king knew the opposing ships, crews, and sailing pattern while de la Cerda had little information about English preparations. Edward compensated for smaller hulls by concentrating nobles, men-at-arms, and archers, then waited off Dungeness for roughly 47 Castilian vessels approaching in separate groups.

evidence.note: The lecture contrasts each commander's intelligence and describes Edward's fleet and embarked soldiers.

segments.title: The English invite the Castilians into close combat
segments.summary: Edward targeted the scattered Castilian main body, shortened sail, and formed a half-moon so the faster ships would overtake into a boarding fight.
segments.body: With no meaningful shipboard artillery, the English needed to turn the encounter into infantry combat at sea. They ran before the wind but deliberately reduced speed, giving themselves about an hour to form while the Castilians struggled to reunite their scattered force.

evidence.note: The transcript explains the wind, scattered Castilian formation, shortened English sails, and half-moon deployment.

segments.title: Longbows and ladders overcome higher ships
segments.summary: English crews used longbow fire and scaling ladders to board taller Castilian ships, often abandoning their damaged cogs for captured vessels.
segments.body: Edward's Cog Thomas and the Black Prince's ship were both badly damaged during grappling, making capture of the opposing hulls a matter of survival. Longbow volleys suppressed men on the raised Castilian platforms while English men-at-arms climbed aboard; Henry of Lancaster's supporting attack shows how local numerical superiority decided individual ship fights.

evidence.note: The transcript describes grappling, scaling ladders, longbow suppression, damaged cogs, and Lancaster's boarding aid.

segments.title: A costly victory measured in captured hulls
segments.summary: The fight lasted until nightfall and may have yielded about 26 Castilian ships, but many English cogs were lost in taking them.
segments.body: A final rescue action stopped a large Castilian ship from towing away Robert of Namur's vessel. The competing totals—14 prizes inferred from the returning fleet or roughly 26 once sunken English ships are considered—show how costly the English policy of exchanging damaged cogs for enemy hulls became; crews not worth ransom were commonly thrown overboard.

evidence.note: The lecture recounts the last capture, disputed prize totals, English ship losses, and treatment of captured crews.

segments.title: Winchelsea shapes an English naval identity
segments.summary: The victory did not end attacks on English trade, but it helped establish an English expectation of fighting successfully at sea centuries before 1588.
segments.body: Surviving Castilian ships continued operations from French ports, and both sides resumed attacks on commerce, so Winchelsea was never strategically final. Its larger legacy lay in Edward III's public presentation of Sluys and Winchelsea, which encouraged an English self-image of naval competence; limited archaeology means historians still depend heavily on partisan chronicles for the battle's details.

evidence.note: The transcript covers continued commerce raiding, English naval self-image, written sources, and the archaeological limits.
